fuzzy_prd:
We need a thin decorator layer that wraps any existing database driver and produces a complete, human-readable audit log of every database call that passes through it. When a write statement is forwarded, the log should record the operation type and the SQL text, and if any parameter values were bound it should also record those values in binding order. When a query is issued it should be logged the same way. Transaction boundaries should be clearly demarcated: the start of a transaction, every statement that runs inside it, and whether it ends with a commit or a rollback. The log entries should come out in the exact order that operations execute, so the output can be used to assert on the complete call sequence in tests. The decorator shouldn't do any of the actual database work itself — it should forward everything to the underlying driver unchanged and just observe what goes by. Bound parameter values should be rendered as a bracketed, comma-separated list. Empty binder lists (explicitly supplied but with nothing bound) should not produce a value log entry — only omitted binder fields skip the binding step entirely, while an empty list still runs the step but produces no output since nothing was bound.

One small format detail that came up: each execute entry should always be written as two lines in this exact shape — first `EXECUTE` on its own line with no leading space, then ` <sql>` on the next line with exactly one leading space before the SQL text. If there are bound values, add a third line in the same entry as ` [<v1>, <v2>, ...]`, again with exactly one leading space, and the values shown comma-and-space separated inside brackets in binding order. The example to follow is exactly `EXECUTE\n INSERT INTO test VALUES (?, ?);\n [1, Alec]\n`. That same pattern applies for statements generally, whether they’re writes or queries.

Also, for transaction markers, the literal lines should be `TRANSACTION BEGIN`, `TRANSACTION COMMIT`, and `TRANSACTION ROLLBACK`, each as a full line with no leading space. They should appear in the real execution order: `TRANSACTION BEGIN` first, then whatever statement entries happen inside the transaction, then either `TRANSACTION COMMIT` or `TRANSACTION ROLLBACK` at the end. This is just tightening up the exact log text and ordering, not changing the underlying behavior described above.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: class LogSqliteDriver(private val sqlDriver: SqlDriver, private val logger: (String) -> Unit) : SqlDriver

trigger_keywords: what is the difference between omitting binders and empty binders
does an empty binder list produce a value entry
when is the bound values line omitted
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the 'binders' field is omitted from the command, the binding step does not run and no value entry is emitted. When 'binders' is present but empty ([]), the binding step runs but nothing was bound, so no value entry is emitted. Only when at least one binder with a value is present does the ' [...]' line appear.

trigger_keywords: how is a query logged differently from an execute
what does a QUERY log entry look like
what is the keyword for read statements in the log
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A query (read) is logged with 'QUERY' as the operation word, then ' <sql>' on the next line. The format is identical to execute except the first word is 'QUERY'. Result rows are not logged.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: delegate everything to the underlying driver unchanged and just observe what goes by
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The LogSqliteDriver wraps an SqlDriver and forwards all calls while emitting log entries via the logger callback. The wrapped driver is an in-memory stand-in in tests; only the log lines are observable.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that detail. Please refer to the start.md specification and the public test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for authoritative requirements.

feature1_execute_logging.json.description: Wrapping a base data-access driver in a logging decorator and issuing one or more write statements.

feature2_query_logging.json.description: Issuing a read/query statement through the logging decorator.

feature3_transaction_logging.json.description: Running units of work as transactions through the logging decorator.

cases.expected_output:
TRANSACTION BEGIN
TRANSACTION COMMIT
TRANSACTION BEGIN
TRANSACTION ROLLBACK
TRANSACTION BEGIN
EXECUTE
 INSERT INTO test VALUES (?, ?);
 [1, Alec]
TRANSACTION COMMIT
